did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically a proposed deep convolutional neural network architecture called CNN-2, to classify skin lesions (melanoma vs. benign) using images from the ISIC Archive.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new deep learning model (CNN-2) for melanoma classification. It details specific methods (training on 2860 ISIC Archive images, 50 epochs, 3-repeated 10-fold stratified cross-validation), reports original results (AUC 0.915 ± 0.02, test AUC 0.9626), and includes a discussion of findings. Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE ICPRS), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Melanoma Cancer Classification using Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
journal: 2023 IEEE 13th International Conference on Pattern Recognition Systems (ICPRS)
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel deep convolutional neural network architecture called CNN-2 to classify skin lesions as either benign or melanoma. The model was trained and evaluated using a balanced dataset of 2,860 dermatoscopic images sourced from the ISIC Archive. Training utilized a three-repeated 10-fold stratified cross-validation scheme over 50 epochs to prevent overfitting and ensure robust evaluation. The CNN-2 model achieved an average AUC score of 0.915 ± 0.02 during cross-validation, performing comparably to more complex models despite fewer training epochs. When tested on a held-out test set, the model demonstrated strong generalization with an AUC of 0.9626, along with 88.85% accuracy, 0.9039 precision, and 0.8692 recall. These results indicate that the proposed CNN-2 is an effective tool for non-invasive melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classification classes in the experiment; 1430 images in the test set and 1430 in the training/validation split were diagnosed as melanoma.
One of the two binary classification classes in the experiment; 1430 images in the test set and 1430 in the training/validation split were diagnosed as benign skin lesions.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into 'benign' or 'melanoma' (cancerous) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the aim is the 'classification of skin lesions' and 'classify skin lesions on a data set of 2860 skin lesions'. The model distinguishes between two discrete classes: benign skin lesions and melanoma-diagnosed lesions. The dataset is balanced with 1430 images per class. The primary metric used is ROC-AUC, and other metrics include accuracy, precision, and recall, all standard for classification tasks. The loss function used is binary cross-entropy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A public imaging resource for developing and testing diagnostic artificial intelligence algorithms, provided by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration. The study used a subset of 2,860 dermatoscopic images from this archive.

use.training: Used for training via a three-repeated 10-fold stratified cross-validation scheme on 90% of the data (2,574 images).
use.validation: Used for validation within the stratified 10-fold cross-validation scheme on the remaining 10% of the training data (286 images) iteratively.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the best model's generalization power on a held-out test set of 286 images (143 benign, 143 melanoma).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's performance and prevent overfitting during the training phase on the dataset of 2860 images.
To compare classification schemes and determine if there were statistically significant differences in ROC-AUC scores between models (CNN-1 and CNN-2) to select the best model.
Used as the cutoff for the Wilcoxon test to determine statistical significance.
To report the variability of the AUC scores during cross-validation (e.g., 0.915 ± 0.02 for CNN-2).

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistically significant difference in the ROC-AUC scores between the compared classification models (CNN-1 and CNN-2) at different epoch counts.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in the ROC-AUC scores between the compared classification models.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The Wilcoxon test was used to determine the best classification model based on statistical significance. For CNN-1, no statistical differences were observed among the models. For CNN-2, the test identified statistically similar models, and the model with the highest AUC (50 epochs) was selected as the best, as it met the criterion of learning faster without sacrificing performance compared to other statistically similar models.

ISIC Archive (Training/Validation via 3-.notes: CNN-1 achieved best AUC of 0.915 at 50 epochs. CNN-2 achieved best AUC of 0.922 at 50 epochs. CNN-2 was selected as the best model because it learned faster without sacrificing performance compared to CNN-1. Statistical differences were assessed using Wilcoxon test with alpha=0.05.

ISIC Archive (Held-out Test Set).notes: The test set contained 286 images (143 benign, 143 melanoma). The model demonstrated strong generalization power.

Comparison with State-of-the-Art (Refere.notes: Comparison is referential due to different databases and validation schemes. CNN-2 accuracy of 0.837 is lower than some pre-trained models but uses significantly fewer parameters and no pre-training.

architecture_details.backbone_structure: Four convolutional blocks followed by fully connected layers

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not explicitly reported, but noted as having 'significantly fewer parameters' compared to pre-trained models like ResNet50

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a fixed size of 128 x 128 pixels
Normalization of color pixel intensity to the range 0 to 1
conclusions: The proposed CNN-2 model successfully classifies skin lesions into benign and melanoma categories, achieving an average AUC of 0.915 ± 0.02 during cross-validation and an AUC of 0.9626 on a held-out test set.
CNN-2 demonstrated superior performance compared to the simpler CNN-1 architecture, learning faster (requiring only 50 epochs) without sacrificing performance.
The CNN-2 model achieved an accuracy of 0.837 on the training/validation data and 0.8885 (88.85%) on the test set, with a precision of 0.9039 and recall of 0.8692.
The model performs comparably to state-of-the-art methods despite not using pre-trained weights or transfer learning, resulting in significantly fewer parameters.
The reduced parameter count of CNN-2 enables efficient implementation on low-resource devices, distinguishing it from previous approaches that rely on complex pre-trained models.
The model's generalization power was confirmed through external test set evaluation, indicating its ability to successfully classify different skin lesion images.

Limitations include the loss of important information (such as pigmentation dispersion) due to resizing large dermatoscopic images to 128x128 pixels, which remains a challenge for specific skin lesions.
Future work will explore new CNN variations, combining the model with transfer learning, and utilizing more extensive databases to reduce generalization error.
Standardized comparisons using the same databases and validation schemes are necessary for future research to ensure fair evaluation against other approaches.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges that resizing large dermatoscopic images to 128x128 pixels causes a loss of important information, specifically the dispersion of skin pigmentation, which is critical for diagnosis.
Comparisons with state-of-the-art methods are purely referential because they utilize different databases and validation schemes, making direct performance comparisons invalid.

The model was trained from scratch without pre-trained weights or transfer learning, resulting in significantly fewer parameters compared to other approaches, which allows for efficient implementation on low-resource devices.
The dataset is balanced (1430 benign, 1430 melanoma), but the authors note that melanoma cases are relatively rare, and using more extensive databases is necessary to reduce generalization error.
The paper does not report data leakage risks explicitly, but the use of a held-out test set (10% of data) for final evaluation helps mitigate concerns about overfitting to the training data.
The study is limited by the lack of standardized comparisons; future work should use the same databases and validation schemes to ensure fair evaluation against other approaches.
